text: This is a video of a laparoscopic pancreas resection.
The patient has a tumor in the distal area of the tail of the pancreas, as well as multiple spleen infarctions.
The surgeon prepares to remove the tail of the pancreas and the spleen en bloc, indicating a simultaneous resection of both structures.
The surgeon begins the operation by incising the left colic flexure, preparing the surgical field for the removal of the pancreatic tail and spleen.
The surgeon cuts the greater omentum to access the omental bursa, revealing the underlying anatomical structures during the laparoscopic pancreatectomy.
The laparoscopic view reveals the liver and pancreas, providing a clear visual of these anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies varicose short gastric arteries originating from the splenic artery, visible in the middle of the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the pancreatic serosa, exposing the underlying pancreatic tissue.
The surgeon identifies the splenic artery positioned above the pancreas, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects beneath the pancreas, revealing the splenic vein, which is clearly visible in the surgical field.
An ultrasound probe is used to examine the pancreas, assessing the planned resection site for the presence of tumor tissue.
The surgeon uses an endogia staple instrument to cut and ligate the splenic artery, which is positioned above the pancreas.
The surgeon uses a stapling device to place two triple rows of staples across the pancreatic tissue, simultaneously cutting the tissue between the rows in a single operation.
The surgeon cuts the splenic vein using a staple instrument, similar to the previous ligation of the splenic artery, with visible rows of staples securing the vessel.
The surgeon uses a larger version of the endo-GIA stapler to divide the pancreas in an area confirmed to be free of tumor tissue.
The distal end of the pancreas is visible in the surgical field, with the tumor located on the right side of the frame.
The surgeon uses the endo-GIA stapling device to cut the varicose arteries, applying staples to secure and divide the tissue.
The stomach is visible on the left side of the frame.
The surgeon dissects the insertion of the spleen from the diaphragm, carefully separating the connective tissue between these structures.
Inflammatory adhesions are visible, with a poorly defined division between the stomach and spleen, positioned against the diaphragm's surface.
Following a challenging dissection, the surgeon releases the stomach from the spleen and uses an endo-GIA stapler to cut the stomach, visibly separating it from the adjacent spleen tissue.
The surgeon completes the separation of the specimen, cutting it loose from surrounding tissues.
The specimen is placed into an endo-catch bag and extracted through an extended umbilical incision.
The video shows staple sutures on the remaining portion of the pancreas, indicating the completion of a previous surgical step involving resection.
The surgical field shows the stomach positioned with the liver visible at the top of the frame, highlighting the spatial relationship between these anatomical structures.
The surgeon covers the remaining pancreas with a TACO cell surgical patch, ensuring the area is protected following the resection.
The surgeon places a drain in the surgical site before proceeding to close the wounds, ensuring proper postoperative fluid management.
The surgeon cuts open the pancreas, revealing a solid yellowish tumor within the tissue.
